content.problemStatement:
After many years of work, Chef has managed to open an art gallery showcasing all his pieces.

There are NNN pieces of art, arranged in a line from left to right.

The iii-th piece from the left has a beauty of AiA_iAi‚Äã.

Each AiA_iAi‚Äã equals either 111 or 222.

A famous art critic is visiting the gallery, and Chef knows that he will move through the gallery as follows.

Initially, the critic will start off by viewing the first art piece. Then, if ppp denotes the index of the piece currently being viewed,


If Ap=1A_p = 1Ap‚Äã=1, the critic will move to piece p+1p+1p+1.
If Ap=2A_p = 2Ap‚Äã=2, the critic will be absorbed in the beauty of what he just saw, and so will move to piece p+2p+2p+2 without viewing piece p+1p+1p+1.
More generally, starting from p=1p = 1p=1, after viewing piece ppp the critic will move to piece p+App + A_pp+Ap‚Äã.

If p+Ap>Np + A_p \gt Np+Ap‚Äã>N, the critic will not view any more pieces.

For an arrangement of art pieces, let f(A)f(A)f(A) denote the number of them that the critic will view.

Chef wants f(A)f(A)f(A) to be as **large** as possible.

To achieve this, he can swap adjacent art pieces.

Functionally, this corresponds to choosing an index iii (1‚â§i<N1 \le i \lt N1‚â§i<N) and performing swap(Ai,Ai+1)\text{swap}(A_i, A_{i+1})swap(Ai‚Äã,Ai+1‚Äã).

Define g(A)g(A)g(A) to be the **minimum number** of such adjacent swaps needed to attain the maximum possible value of f(A)f(A)f(A).

You must answer QQQ queries.

Each query will provide you with two integers LLL and RRR, and you must compute the value of g(A[L,‚Ä¶,R])g(A[L, \ldots, R])g(A[L,‚Ä¶,R]), where A[L,‚Ä¶,R]=[AL,AL+1,‚Ä¶,AR]A[L, \ldots, R] = [A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R]A[L,‚Ä¶,R]=[AL‚Äã,AL+1‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AR‚Äã] denotes the subarray of AAA from index LLL to index RRR, inclusive.

Note that each query treats the respective subarray as an independent array; in particular you are not allowed to swap a piece inside the subarray with a piece outside the subarray.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
The next QQQ lines describe the queries. The iii-th of them contains two space-integers LiL_iLi‚Äã and RiR_iRi‚Äã, denoting the bounds for the iii-th query.




Output Format
For each query, output on a new line the answer: the minimum number of adjacent swaps needed to maximize f(A[L,‚Ä¶,R])f(A[L, \ldots, R])f(A[L,‚Ä¶,R]) for the given L,RL, RL,R.
constraints: The sum of NNN and the sum of QQQ over all test cases each won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 333: There are two queries.

Query 111: L=1,R=5L = 1, R = 5L=1,R=5
This is the entire array, and the answer is 111 because one optimal final configuration is [1,2,1,1,2][1, 2, 1, 1, 2][1,2,1,1,2] for which f(A)=4f(A) = 4f(A)=4. Getting to this configuration requires one swap of adjacent elements.
Query 222: L=2,R=4L = 2, R = 4L=2,R=4
This gives us the subarray [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2].
It has a score of 222, and swaps cannot increase this so no swaps are necessary; the answer is 000.
